An accidental cross join (omitted condition or trivially-true condition) silently multiplies row counts — joining two 500K-row tables yields 250 BILLION rows, which can crash a cluster
Cross joins always raise an error
Cross joins fail silently and produce empty results
questions.correctAnswer: An accidental cross join (omitted condition or trivially-true condition) silently multiplies row counts — joining two 500K-row tables yields 250 BILLION rows, which can crash a cluster
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: cross joins are output multipliers — `left × right`. Spark won't stop you from joining on `lit(True)`. The signature symptom: a result far larger than either input. Always sanity-check row counts after a join.

questions.question: How are semi joins and anti joins different from inner/left/full joins?
options: They are slower equivalents
They are FILTERS — they use the right DataFrame only to decide which left-side rows to keep (semi) or drop (anti), and add NO columns from the right
They always drop nulls
They are aliases of inner and outer joins
questions.correctAnswer: They are FILTERS — they use the right DataFrame only to decide which left-side rows to keep (semi) or drop (anti), and add NO columns from the right
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: semi answers 'which left rows have a match?' (keeps them); anti answers 'which left rows have NO match?' (keeps those). Output columns come only from the left side — they're filters disguised as joins.

questions.question: When does column ambiguity (`Reference 'region' is ambiguous`) typically occur after a join?
options: Whenever both sides have any column with the same name, regardless of join syntax
When you join on a Column expression like `df_a['region'] == df_b['region']` — both `region` columns survive into the result. Joining on a string `on='region'` collapses them automatically
Only on left joins
When the DataFrames have different schemas
questions.correctAnswer: When you join on a Column expression like `df_a['region'] == df_b['region']` — both `region` columns survive into the result. Joining on a string `on='region'` collapses them automatically
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: `on='region'` keeps a single region column; `df_a.region == df_b.region` keeps both. Fix by joining on string keys when possible, aliasing the DataFrames, or dropping the duplicate column right after the join.

questions.question: What is a non-equi join?
options: A join that intentionally produces no results
A join whose condition uses a comparison other than `=` — `BETWEEN`, `<`, `>` — typically for range lookups (e.g. matching readings to billing tier brackets)
A join on multiple columns
A self-join
questions.correctAnswer: A join whose condition uses a comparison other than `=` — `BETWEEN`, `<`, `>` — typically for range lookups (e.g. matching readings to billing tier brackets)
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: equi-joins (`=`) cover 90% of cases. Non-equi joins handle ranges — e.g. matching `daily_kw` to a tier table where each tier defines `min_kw <= reading < max_kw`. They are more expensive to plan because hash-based join strategies don't apply.

questions.question: What is NordGrid's defensive habit after every join in a multi-step join pipeline?
options: Cache the result of every join
Assert (or otherwise verify) the row count — a left join shouldn't change the left side's row count, so any drift is an immediate signal of a bad join condition or duplicate keys
Re-cast every column
Sort the result
questions.correctAnswer: Assert (or otherwise verify) the row count — a left join shouldn't change the left side's row count, so any drift is an immediate signal of a bad join condition or duplicate keys
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: row-count assertions are the cheapest debugging tool you have. A silent fan-out from duplicate keys, or silent loss from a bad inner join, becomes an explicit failure where it happens, not 4 transformations later.

questions.question: Why is a broadcast join much cheaper than a shuffle hash / sort-merge join when one side is small?
options: Broadcast joins use less CPU per row
Broadcast sends the SMALL DataFrame to every executor in full; the LARGE DataFrame is matched in place, with no shuffle of the large side at all
Broadcast joins always use one executor
Broadcast joins skip the join condition
questions.correctAnswer: Broadcast sends the SMALL DataFrame to every executor in full; the LARGE DataFrame is matched in place, with no shuffle of the large side at all
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: shuffling a multi-billion-row table is the expensive part. If the right side fits in executor memory (default threshold ~10 MB), Spark broadcasts it and does the join locally on each partition of the large table — no large-side shuffle.

questions.question: Does `spark.read.parquet('path/')` immediately load data into memory?
options: Yes — it reads the entire file into the driver
No — it returns a DataFrame that REFERENCES the files; the actual read happens when an action triggers, with column pruning and predicate pushdown applied
It loads only the schema and discards the data
It depends on file size
questions.correctAnswer: No — it returns a DataFrame that REFERENCES the files; the actual read happens when an action triggers, with column pruning and predicate pushdown applied
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: `spark.read` is lazy like every other DataFrame operation. It records intent. The reader picks up filters and column lists from later transformations and reads only what's needed when you finally trigger execution.

questions.question: Why does `inferSchema=True` on CSV cost so much?
options: Spark always re-encodes the file when inferring
Spark must scan the entire file TWICE — once to guess column types, then again to actually read with those types
It triples the storage requirement
It only works in cluster mode
questions.correctAnswer: Spark must scan the entire file TWICE — once to guess column types, then again to actually read with those types
questions.explanation: Lesson 02 warning: CSV has no type metadata, so `inferSchema=True` does a full pass to guess types and a second to load. On a 500 GB archive that doubles read cost. In production, supply a `StructType` schema explicitly.

questions.question: What does `spark.read.json('path/')` expect by default?
options: A single huge JSON document at the path
JSON Lines (newline-delimited JSON) — one complete JSON object per line; the standard format for log/event data
A YAML file
A binary serialized JSON
questions.correctAnswer: JSON Lines (newline-delimited JSON) — one complete JSON object per line; the standard format for log/event data
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: JSONL is the default for streaming/event ingestion. For multi-line JSON documents (a single object spanning many lines), set `option('multiLine', True)`. Nested objects come back as StructType columns.

questions.question: Why does Parquet rarely need an explicit schema when reading?
options: Parquet files don't have a schema
Parquet files embed the schema (column names, types, nullability) in the file metadata — Spark reads it directly with no inference required
Spark always infers Parquet schemas from the filename
Parquet uses the same schema for every dataset
questions.correctAnswer: Parquet files embed the schema (column names, types, nullability) in the file metadata — Spark reads it directly with no inference required
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: Parquet is self-describing. The schema travels with the data, so the reader gets correct types — including dates and nullability — for free. This is one reason Parquet is the standard for processed analytical data.

questions.question: Why is schema inference dangerous in long-lived production pipelines?
options: Inference is always wrong

Inference depends on the sample's content — if the source starts producing values that change the inferred type (e.g. `daily_kw` had only integers, then a row arrives with `1250.5`), downstream code that assumed the old type breaks silently
Inference works only the first time a pipeline runs
Inferred schemas can't be saved to Parquet
questions.correctAnswer: Inference depends on the sample's content — if the source starts producing values that change the inferred type (e.g. `daily_kw` had only integers, then a row arrives with `1250.5`), downstream code that assumed the old type breaks silently
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: a real NordGrid incident. inferSchema is non-deterministic across data drift. An explicit StructType pins the contract: if the data actually changes, you get an explicit error instead of silent breakage at the analytics layer.

questions.question: What's the biggest risk of using `mode('append')` for an idempotent daily pipeline?
options: It overwrites yesterday's data
If the pipeline runs twice for the same day (retry after a transient failure), the second run appends a duplicate set of rows — the output silently doubles for that day
It is slower than overwrite
It only works for Parquet
questions.correctAnswer: If the pipeline runs twice for the same day (retry after a transient failure), the second run appends a duplicate set of rows — the output silently doubles for that day
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: append doesn't dedupe. For idempotency, either (a) use `overwrite` partitioned by date so a re-run replaces just that day, or (b) write to a staging path and use a transactional copy. Append + retries = duplicates.

questions.question: How do you control how many output files a `df.write.parquet(...)` produces?
options: Set `option('maxFiles', n)`
It is determined by the number of DataFrame partitions at write time — use `coalesce(n)` to reduce or `repartition(n)` to redistribute before writing
Spark always writes exactly one file
Output files are determined by the cluster manager
questions.correctAnswer: It is determined by the number of DataFrame partitions at write time — use `coalesce(n)` to reduce or `repartition(n)` to redistribute before writing
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: one output file per partition. coalesce(n) reduces partitions without a shuffle (good when going down). repartition(n) reshuffles — necessary when going up or when partition sizes are unbalanced.

questions.question: Why is `partitionBy('meter_id')` a bad idea on a 500,000-meter table?
options: Spark cannot partitionBy on string columns
It creates 500,000 directories — the small-files problem; each directory holds tiny files and metadata operations dominate read time, often more expensive than reading the data
Partitioned writes only support numeric columns
PartitionBy doubles the storage cost
questions.correctAnswer: It creates 500,000 directories — the small-files problem; each directory holds tiny files and metadata operations dominate read time, often more expensive than reading the data
questions.explanation: Lesson 08 warning: partition columns should have LOW cardinality. Region (4 values) is great. Date (~1K values) is fine if you query by date often. Meter_id (500K) blows up directory listing time and turns a 5-second scan into 5 minutes of metadata operations.

questions.question: What's the fundamental layout difference between CSV and Parquet?
options: CSV is faster for analytical queries
CSV is row-oriented (each row's columns sit together on disk); Parquet is column-oriented (each column's values are grouped together), enabling column pruning and per-column compression
CSV uses Unicode and Parquet uses ASCII
There is no difference; they are aliases
questions.correctAnswer: CSV is row-oriented (each row's columns sit together on disk); Parquet is column-oriented (each column's values are grouped together), enabling column pruning and per-column compression
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: row-oriented favors transactional writes (append a row, you write all columns together). Column-oriented favors analytical reads (read just the columns you need, compress each column with the codec best suited to its values). This is why Parquet dominates analytics.

questions.question: In NordGrid's pipeline, why does CSV stay at the Bronze layer while Parquet is used for Silver and Gold?
options: CSV is faster than Parquet

Source systems produce CSV, so Bronze accepts it as-is; once cleaned, Silver and Gold use Parquet for the schema-bearing, column-pruned, compressed analytical layout that downstream readers benefit from
Parquet cannot be ingested
Spark requires this exact arrangement
questions.correctAnswer: Source systems produce CSV, so Bronze accepts it as-is; once cleaned, Silver and Gold use Parquet for the schema-bearing, column-pruned, compressed analytical layout that downstream readers benefit from
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: format follows the pipeline stage. Bronze prioritizes faithful capture of whatever the source produced (CSV/JSON). Silver/Gold prioritize fast analytical reads, where Parquet's column layout, compression, and self-describing schema pay off.

questions.question: Why are functions from `pyspark.sql.functions` (like `upper`, `year`, `round`) so much faster than wrapping the same logic in a Python UDF?
options: They run on dedicated GPUs
Spark compiles them to JVM bytecode and executes them directly on the executors; the Python layer is only involved when you build the plan, not when rows are processed
They cache results between calls
There is no performance difference
questions.correctAnswer: Spark compiles them to JVM bytecode and executes them directly on the executors; the Python layer is only involved when you build the plan, not when rows are processed
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: built-ins are JVM-native. Each row stays in the JVM through the whole pipeline. UDFs serialize each row to Python and back per row — much slower and Catalyst can't reason about the operation.

questions.question: When source data packs multiple values into a delimited string (e.g. `'NORTH-METER-001'`), what's the cleanest way to extract the parts?
options: Always reach for regexp_extract with a complex pattern
Use `split('-')` to produce an array, then `element_at(arr, 1/2/3)` (1-indexed) to pull each piece — readable and avoids regex
Loop over rows in Python
Cast the column to a struct
questions.correctAnswer: Use `split('-')` to produce an array, then `element_at(arr, 1/2/3)` (1-indexed) to pull each piece — readable and avoids regex
questions.explanation: Lesson 02: split + element_at handles most delimited-string parsing without writing regex. Reserve regexp_replace/regexp_extract for genuinely irregular patterns.

questions.question: When dates arrive as strings in a non-ISO format (e.g. `'01/March/2026'`), what's the right way to convert them to DateType?
options: Use `cast('date')` — it auto-detects every format
Use `to_date(col, 'dd/MMMM/yyyy')` — `cast('date')` only handles the ISO format `YYYY-MM-DD`
Use string manipulation to rebuild ISO format first, then cast
It's not possible without a UDF
questions.correctAnswer: Use `to_date(col, 'dd/MMMM/yyyy')` — `cast('date')` only handles the ISO format `YYYY-MM-DD`
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: cast assumes ISO-8601 dates. For any other format, `to_date()` (or `to_timestamp()`) takes an explicit format string. Parse once at the Bronze→Silver boundary; then DateType all the way through.

questions.question: What's the difference between `greatest(col('a'), col('b'), col('c'))` and `max(col('a'))`?
options: They are equivalent — both return the same value
`greatest()` compares values ACROSS COLUMNS in the SAME row (returns the max of a, b, c per row); `max()` aggregates DOWN ROWS (returns the max of column a across the whole DataFrame)
`greatest()` works only for numbers; `max()` for any type
`max()` is faster
questions.correctAnswer: `greatest()` compares values ACROSS COLUMNS in the SAME row (returns the max of a, b, c per row); `max()` aggregates DOWN ROWS (returns the max of column a across the whole DataFrame)
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: greatest/least are row-level — same row, multiple columns. max/min are aggregate functions — same column, multiple rows. They look similar but live on different axes; mixing them up is a classic confusion.

questions.question: What happens to the row count when you `explode()` an array column?
options: Row count stays the same
Rows MULTIPLY — one row with an N-element array becomes N rows, with the other columns duplicated; useful for flattening hourly samples but easy to overlook size impact
Row count drops to one summary row
Spark refuses to explode arrays larger than 100
questions.correctAnswer: Rows MULTIPLY — one row with an N-element array becomes N rows, with the other columns duplicated; useful for flattening hourly samples but easy to overlook size impact
questions.explanation: Lesson 05 warning: explode is row-multiplying. 500,000 meters × 24 hourly samples = 12 million rows after exploding. Plan for the size; pre-filter the array if you don't need every element.

questions.question: What does `coalesce(col('primary'), col('backup'), lit(0.0))` return?
options: Always 0.0
The FIRST non-null value among the arguments — primary if non-null, otherwise backup, otherwise 0.0
All three values concatenated
An error if primary is null
questions.correctAnswer: The FIRST non-null value among the arguments — primary if non-null, otherwise backup, otherwise 0.0
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: coalesce builds a fallback chain across COLUMNS. Useful when readings come from multiple sources (primary feed, backup cell, manual entry). Distinct from `fillna({col: 0})`, which only fills nulls in one specific column with one literal.

questions.question: What's NordGrid's convention about WHERE in the pipeline null handling should happen?
options: Wherever it's convenient
All null handling is done at the Bronze→Silver boundary — by the time data reaches Silver, every column has well-defined non-null semantics, so downstream code never has to special-case nulls again
Nulls are passed through every layer untouched
Nulls are handled at the dashboard layer only
questions.correctAnswer: All null handling is done at the Bronze→Silver boundary — by the time data reaches Silver, every column has well-defined non-null semantics, so downstream code never has to special-case nulls again
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: pushing null handling into one boundary eliminates an entire class of recurring bugs. The Silver contract becomes: 'columns are clean, nulls are explicit and intentional, everyone downstream can rely on it.'

questions.question: How does a window function like `lag(col('daily_kw'), 1)` differ from `groupBy('meter_id').agg(lag(...))`?
options: They are the same operation
Window functions ADD a column without reducing the row count — every row gets the lagged value computed within its window. groupBy().agg() COLLAPSES rows into one row per group
Window functions only work on numeric columns
Window functions are only available in SQL
questions.correctAnswer: Window functions ADD a column without reducing the row count — every row gets the lagged value computed within its window. groupBy().agg() COLLAPSES rows into one row per group
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: window functions preserve every input row and decorate it with values computed across a window of related rows. groupBy reduces; window decorates. Day-over-day deltas, running totals, and rank-within-group all need windows, not groupBy.

questions.question: Why are Python UDFs typically 10–100× slower than equivalent built-in expressions?
options: Spark deliberately throttles UDFs

UDFs force every row to cross the JVM↔Python boundary (serialize → run Python → serialize back); built-ins stay in the JVM. Catalyst also can't reason about UDF logic, so optimizations like predicate pushdown are skipped
UDFs run on a single executor
There is no performance difference
questions.correctAnswer: UDFs force every row to cross the JVM↔Python boundary (serialize → run Python → serialize back); built-ins stay in the JVM. Catalyst also can't reason about UDF logic, so optimizations like predicate pushdown are skipped
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: UDFs pay a per-row serialization cost twice and become opaque to the optimizer. Always check pyspark.sql.functions before writing one. Use `when().otherwise()` chains for conditional logic — they're built-ins, fast, and Catalyst-aware.

questions.question: Order these from FASTEST to SLOWEST: built-in Column functions, expr() SQL strings, Python UDFs.
options: UDF > expr > built-in
Built-in functions ≈ expr() (both compile to the same plan) > Python UDF (much slower due to JVM↔Python serialization)
expr > UDF > built-in
All three perform identically
questions.correctAnswer: Built-in functions ≈ expr() (both compile to the same plan) > Python UDF (much slower due to JVM↔Python serialization)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the performance hierarchy is built-in ≈ expr() ≫ UDF. expr() parses to the same Catalyst plan as the Column API. UDFs sit far below because of cross-language overhead. Decision order: built-in first, expr() if SQL is clearer, UDF only if no other option.

questions.question: Why must you call `df.createOrReplaceTempView('readings')` before writing `spark.sql('SELECT * FROM readings')`?
options: Spark SQL only works in cluster mode
SQL queries reference tables by NAME — a Python DataFrame variable doesn't give SQL a name to bind to. Registering a view exposes the DataFrame under that name in the session catalog
Spark SQL requires Hive metastore configuration
The DataFrame must first be saved to disk
questions.correctAnswer: SQL queries reference tables by NAME — a Python DataFrame variable doesn't give SQL a name to bind to. Registering a view exposes the DataFrame under that name in the session catalog
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: `createOrReplaceTempView` is the bridge — it gives the DataFrame a name SQL can bind to. The DataFrame itself isn't materialized; the view is just a logical alias.

questions.question: What does `spark.sql('SELECT region, sum(daily_kw) FROM readings GROUP BY region')` return?
options: A Python list of tuples
A standard PySpark DataFrame — you can chain DataFrame transformations on it, register it as another view, or write it to Parquet
A SQL string for further parsing
A Pandas DataFrame
questions.correctAnswer: A standard PySpark DataFrame — you can chain DataFrame transformations on it, register it as another view, or write it to Parquet
questions.explanation: Lesson 02: spark.sql returns a DataFrame just like spark.read does. SQL and the DataFrame API freely interoperate — you can write a SQL aggregation and then chain `.filter()` on the result.

questions.question: If SQL and the DataFrame API produce the same execution plan, why support both?
options: Compatibility with legacy SQL servers

Each is clearer in different scenarios — SQL for declarative analytics queries (joins + groupBy + having), DataFrame API for procedural ETL (step-by-step casts, filters, conditionals); Catalyst compiles both to the same internal plan
DataFrame API is always faster
SQL is required for cluster mode
questions.correctAnswer: Each is clearer in different scenarios — SQL for declarative analytics queries (joins + groupBy + having), DataFrame API for procedural ETL (step-by-step casts, filters, conditionals); Catalyst compiles both to the same internal plan
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: same engine, two front doors. Choose by READABILITY for the task — analytics teams reading SQL fluently; engineers wiring up testable, IDE-aware ETL using the DataFrame API.

questions.question: What's the practical difference between a temporary view and a global temporary view?
options: Global temp views are stored permanently on disk

A temp view (`createOrReplaceTempView`) is visible only inside the SparkSession that created it. A global temp view (`createOrReplaceGlobalTempView`) lives in the special `global_temp` schema and is visible to ALL sessions in the same Spark application
Global temp views are faster
There is no difference
questions.correctAnswer: A temp view (`createOrReplaceTempView`) is visible only inside the SparkSession that created it. A global temp view (`createOrReplaceGlobalTempView`) lives in the special `global_temp` schema and is visible to ALL sessions in the same Spark application
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: scope is the only meaningful difference. Default to temp views — the smaller scope avoids cross-pipeline collisions. Reach for global temp views only when you genuinely need cross-session sharing.

questions.question: What does NordGrid's hybrid pipeline pattern look like?
options: All SQL or all DataFrame API — never mixed

DataFrame API for the procedural Bronze→Silver work (load, cast, filter, rename), then register the Silver DataFrame as a view and use SQL for the declarative analytical Gold queries (joins, groupBy, aggregations)
SQL for ingestion, DataFrame API for output
Always start with SQL and convert to DataFrame at the end
questions.correctAnswer: DataFrame API for the procedural Bronze→Silver work (load, cast, filter, rename), then register the Silver DataFrame as a view and use SQL for the declarative analytical Gold queries (joins, groupBy, aggregations)
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: each stage gets the tool that's clearest there. Procedural ETL — DataFrame API. Declarative analytics — SQL. Mixing freely is the productive pattern; forcing one or the other costs readability.

questions.question: What's the SQL equivalent of method chaining `df.filter(...).groupBy(...).agg(...)`?
options: Stored procedures
Common Table Expressions (CTEs) — `WITH step1 AS (...), step2 AS (...) SELECT ... FROM step2` — each CTE is one named transformation, readable top-to-bottom
Triggers
Subqueries with no alias
questions.correctAnswer: Common Table Expressions (CTEs) — `WITH step1 AS (...), step2 AS (...) SELECT ... FROM step2` — each CTE is one named transformation, readable top-to-bottom
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: CTEs let SQL flow as a step-by-step pipeline instead of nested subqueries. Each step gets a name; later steps reference earlier ones. The same intent as DataFrame chaining, expressed in SQL.

questions.question: How does `UPPER(region)` in Spark SQL relate to `upper(col('region'))` in the DataFrame API?
options: They are different functions with different semantics
They are the same operation — Spark SQL functions and DataFrame API functions compile to the same internal expression; only the surface syntax differs
SQL UPPER is slower
DataFrame `upper` only handles ASCII
questions.correctAnswer: They are the same operation — Spark SQL functions and DataFrame API functions compile to the same internal expression; only the surface syntax differs
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: every function category — string, date, numeric, conditional, aggregation — has parallel API and SQL forms backed by the same JVM implementation. Same engine, different syntax.

questions.question: Why is `spark.catalog.tableExists('my_view')` useful in a SQL-heavy pipeline?
options: It speeds up queries

Defensive programming — verify required views are registered before running a query, so a missing-view bug fails fast with a clear message instead of a confusing SQL parse error deep inside a long script
Spark requires it before every SELECT
It deletes the view if it exists
questions.correctAnswer: Defensive programming — verify required views are registered before running a query, so a missing-view bug fails fast with a clear message instead of a confusing SQL parse error deep inside a long script
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: catalog checks are cheap and turn 'view missing in step 7 of 12' into 'view missing — pre-flight check failed.' Same pattern as schema validation: spend a few lines to fail clearly at the boundary.

questions.question: Which factor matters MOST when choosing between SQL and the DataFrame API at NordGrid?
options: Personal preference of the engineer writing the code
Team consistency — whichever interface is chosen, the codebase should use it consistently for similar operations so reviewers and on-call engineers don't have to re-learn the conventions every file
Always SQL because analysts read it
Always DataFrame API because it is faster
questions.correctAnswer: Team consistency — whichever interface is chosen, the codebase should use it consistently for similar operations so reviewers and on-call engineers don't have to re-learn the conventions every file
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: convention beats preference. A consistent codebase is easier to review, easier to debug at 3 AM, and easier to onboard into. Pick a rule (e.g. ETL = API, analytics = SQL) and stick to it.

questions.question: After completing the junior PySpark track, what foundation has been built?
options: Only SQL syntax

Every operation a junior data engineer uses daily — SparkSession, DataFrames, selecting/filtering, transformations, aggregations, joins, file I/O, built-in functions, and SQL — all backed by the same Catalyst engine
Only the basics of distributed computing theory, no practical skills
Only knowledge of the NordGrid scenario
questions.correctAnswer: Every operation a junior data engineer uses daily — SparkSession, DataFrames, selecting/filtering, transformations, aggregations, joins, file I/O, built-in functions, and SQL — all backed by the same Catalyst engine
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the junior track covers the full daily toolkit. Every later module (mid-level, senior) builds on these primitives — performance tuning, streaming, advanced patterns — but the foundation is the same set of ten modules you just completed.
